vulnerabilities.description: The article upload form accepts .php files without apparent server-side validation. Upload returned a success message, and an upload directory exists at /posts/uploads/ (403 when listing), indicating files are stored and may be executable if PHP is enabled there. Not executed; only confirmed acceptance.

vulnerabilities.type: Potential Local File Inclusion / Path Traversal
vulnerabilities.description: The id parameter controls file-based content. A traversal payload returned 'Directory traversal attempt detected!', implying custom blacklist filtering. This suggests file inclusion logic that may be bypassable via encoding/alternate payloads. Not exploited.
Server reveals Apache/2.4.54 (Debian) and X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.33. PHP 7.4 is end-of-life and may contain known vulnerabilities; version disclosure aids attackers.
Responses lack common security headers (e.g., Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options), increasing exposure to XSS and clickjacking.
Forms do not display anti-CSRF tokens in the HTML, suggesting potential CSRF vulnerabilities on state-changing endpoints.
